Activate Using Occurrence and Entity Parameters

When you activate a 4GL service, you have the possibility to pass parameters of type OCCURRENCE and ENTITY. The same possibility exists when activating a 3GL function, although 3GL code has no notion of a Uniface occurrence or entity.

You can use these data types in your signature, but on the 3GL side they must be simulated by using a struct (for an occurrence) or an array of structs (for an entity).

Using an Occurrence Parameter

An occurrence parameter corresponds to one occurrence of your entity. What Uniface passes to your C routine is a pointer to a fixed-size buffer that contains the data as laid out in Uniface. Typically, you will map this to a C struct containing the relevant fields, although you could also use an array of the correct size. This may take some experimenting, and it might get complicated if you are using Uniface-specific data types or packing codes. There is no tool to generate this struct from the signature.

A simple example follows. Suppose you have an entity MYENT with these fields:

  • CODE—Data type string, Packing code C3
  • DESC—Data type string, Packing code C8

You have defined a C signature CSIG with an operation CStore having a MYENT occurrence parameter. Your C code would typically look like this:

typedef struct
{
char CODE [3];
char DESC [8];
} MYENT;
void CStore (MYENT * pOcc)
{
printf("code = '%.3s'/ndesc = '%.8s'/n", pOcc->CODE, pOcc->DESC);
}

In your Uniface application, your current MYENT occurrence has the values:

  • CODE—U
  • DESC—Unknown

Calling your 3GL function with the statement:

activate "CSIG".Cstore("MYENT")

produces the output:

code = 'U '
desc = 'Unknown '

Note that you get fixed-length, space-padded data, without C null-terminators. Because this interface is based on a record layout, there is no way to know how long the actual data in a field is.

Using an Entity Parameter

For an entity parameter, Uniface will pass to your 3GL function a pointer to an array of occurrence structs. The size of this array, that is, the number of occurrences passed, is specified in the definition of the entity parameter in the signature (the field ‘No. of occurrences’ in the details of the entity parameter). There is no separator or padding between the individual occurrences.

A simple example follows. In the above signature CSIG, define another operation, CStore3, this time with a MYENT entity parameter, and specify 3 occurrences. Your C code would typically look like this:

#define NOCCS 3
typedef struct
{
char CODE [3];
char DESC [8];
} MYENT;
void CStore3 (MYENT pEnt[])
{
int i;
for ( i=0; i<NOCCS; i++ )
{
printf("code = '%.3s' desc = '%.8s'/n", pEnt[i].CODE, pEnt[i].DESC);
}
}

In your Uniface application, your current MYENT hitlist has these values:

Table 1. MYENT hitlist for 3GL call-out

Code

DESC

U

Unknown

T

To do

C

Cancelled

Calling your 3GL function CStore3 with the statement:

activate "CSIG".Cstore3("MYENT")

will pass to Cstore3 a pointer to the following block of data:

"U Unknown T To do C Canceled"

which will produce the output:

code = 'U ' desc = 'Unknown '
code = 'T ' desc = 'To do '
code = 'C ' desc = 'Canceled'

Notes

There are some points to keep in mind when you work with occurrence and entity parameters. For more information, see Define Operation Parameters in a Signature.
